what_it_does: Acceptance test for the RHM's native tool-calling path: it exercises tool_call-to-action mapping, dispatcher whitelist enforcement, affordance sets per mode and context, capability-gate fail-loud refusal for non-tool models, empty-content tool-call confirmation replies, and mode-discipline gating at dispatch.
observations: Uses a global PASS counter and a check(label, cond) helper to assert conditions.
Builds OpenAI-shape tool_calls via tool_call(name, arguments) with JSON-string arguments.
Tests both direct _dispatch_rhm_action calls and the full suite.chat() path.
Monkey-patches fabric.client.complete_with_tools and suite._model_supports_tools to simulate model responses.
Verifies that forbidden verbs like delete are refused end-to-end and nodes survive.
Checks available_verbs for modes listening, watch-and-react, walkthrough, focus, background, text-only, decide-for-me, and off.
Asserts _rhm_tools derives from available_verbs plus a suggest meta-offer tool.
Asserts RHM_VERBS derives from RHM_VERB_SPECS and preserves core-7 order plus configure/load_voice/unload_voice.
Simulates a non-tool model (nomic-embed-text) and verifies zero model calls and a legible refusal.
Simulates empty assistant content plus a run tool_call and checks a non-blank confirmation reply.
Tests mode-discipline: build is whitelisted but not offered in watch-and-react, so it is refused at dispatch.
standouts: End-to-end whitelist enforcement through native tool_calls: a delete tool_call is mapped to an action but refused by the dispatcher.
Mode-discipline as a real dispatch gate: a whitelisted verb that is not offered in the current mode is refused.
Capability-gate fail-loud for non-tool models with no fallback and no model invocation.
Empty-content tool_call still yields a non-blank user-facing confirmation reply.
conventions: Follows an acceptance-test pattern with setup, assertions, and tempfile cleanup.
Uses try/finally to restore monkey-patched functions.
Breaks typical encapsulation by assigning lambdas to instance methods for test control.
Uses inline __import__('json').dumps calls to build argument strings.
Relies on exact reply substrings for behavioral assertions.
concerns: Heavy reliance on private implementation details (complete_with_tools, _model_supports_tools) makes the test brittle.
Exact reply-substring assertions may break with wording changes.
Global PASS counter and generic check() helper obscure the precise failure context.
Mixes unit-level dispatcher checks with integration-level chat() stubs in one file.
Hardcodes model names and capability assumptions that may drift.
notes: A behavioral contract test that pins down how native tool-calling, affordance narrowing, and multi-layer gating should behave in the RHM.
questions: What is the complete definition of RHM_VERBS, RHM_VERB_DESC, and RHM_VERB_CLASS?
How does _json_obj_to_action handle malformed or missing tool arguments?
What is the exact signature and behavior of fabric.client.complete_with_tools?
What does the suggest tool do and how is it handled at dispatch?
How are mode/context predicates implemented inside available_verbs?
What is the intended behavior when multiple tool_calls are returned?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the content: it tests native tool-calling dispatch, affordance correctness, whitelist enforcement, capability gating, empty-content replies, and mode-discipline.
apparent_themes: affordance narrowing by mode and context
intention_signals: The file is trying to lock in that the RHM acts through native tool-calls, the dispatcher whitelist remains the ultimate gate, offered verbs are mode/context-sensitive, non-tool models are refused visibly, and tool-only assistant turns still produce user-facing confirmations.
novelty: It combines capability-gate fail-loud, mode-discipline at dispatch, and empty-content confirmation behavior into a single end-to-end acceptance test.
contribution: A concrete acceptance test that documents expected RHM tool-call behavior, expected affordance sets per mode, and expected multi-layer gating for regression protection.
summary_for_embedding: An acceptance test that verifies the RHM's native tool-calling dispatch, affordance sets, whitelist enforcement, capability-gate refusal, empty-content confirmation, and mode-discipline gating.
intention_for_embedding: To pin down and protect the RHM's behavior when using native tool-calling with mode-aware affordances and layered safety gates.

symbols.does: Assertion helper that increments a global pass counter on success.
symbols.description: check(label, cond) asserts cond, increments the global PASS counter, and prints an ok message; on failure it raises with the label.

symbols.does: Constructs an OpenAI-shape tool_call object with JSON-string arguments.
symbols.description: tool_call(name, arguments) returns a dict with id, type, and function fields, serializing arguments to JSON so it matches the API shape used by _json_obj_to_action.

symbols.does: Stub that returns empty assistant content plus a run tool_call.
symbols.description: _stub_run(*a, **k) simulates a native-tool model that emits no prose and only a run tool_call, used to test empty-content confirmation behavior.

symbols.does: Stub that returns empty assistant content plus a delete tool_call.
symbols.description: _stub_delete(*a, **k) simulates a model attempting a forbidden delete via native tool_call, used to test end-to-end whitelist refusal through chat().

symbols.does: Stub that returns empty assistant content plus a build tool_call.
symbols.description: _stub_build(*a, **k) simulates a model attempting a build tool_call in watch-and-react mode, used to test mode-discipline refusal at dispatch.

symbols.does: Stub that returns empty assistant content plus a show tool_call.
symbols.description: _stub_show(*a, **k) simulates a model emitting a show tool_call in watch-and-react mode, used to verify that offered verbs still dispatch normally.

symbols.does: Stub that returns empty assistant content plus a second delete tool_call.
symbols.description: _stub_del2(*a, **k) simulates another forbidden delete attempt in watch-and-react mode, used to confirm E6 whitelist enforcement still holds through the mode gate.
